02 Four Types of Meetings - YouTube.mp4
There are four types of meetings that all entrepreneurs should leverage when architecting. They're companies meeting rhythm, and they are. Number one: Planning meetings? Number two, review meetings. Number three, check-in meetings. And number four, ad hoc meetings. Now, planning meetings, as the name suggests, are highly intentional, day-long, and even multi-day strategic planning sessions. This is the most important and valuable time a team will spend together because this is where goals are set, priorities are established, and projects are greenlit. Planning meetings typically have a quarterly cadence, although some types of planning meetings may only need to occur once every one to three years. We're going to talk about that one. Now, review meetings, again, as the name suggests, are designed to review the progress that the company is making towards the goal.
It's set during the previous planning meetings. These review meetings, which are typically held monthly, can last anywhere from 90 minutes to a full day. And they are designed to give leaders— information and insights they need to make any necessary pivots. Check-in meetings are designed to allow for rapid sharing of information. These kinds of meetings are typical. Held weekly, they include team scorecard meetings, company all-hands meetings, and employee-manager one-on-ones. While big decisions are sometimes made during check-in meetings, they're primarily designed for information sharing in preparation for a more in-depth review meeting. The last meeting type is the ad hoc meeting. Now, these are the one-off meetings that everyone seems to hate, but when done right, ad hoc meetings can include project kickoff meetings, brainstorming meetings, and what we call black swan meetings, which are the meetings you need to have when the 'you-know-what' hits the fan. But with the exception of project kickoff meetings, ad hoc meetings should be deployed very infrequently. So if you find yourself having a lot of ad hoc meetings, it's usually a sign that something didn't go quite right at the planning meeting level. Now that you understand the four basic meeting types, let's discuss how you can incorporate each into your company's meeting rhythm.
